summary: McKenzie shares hard-won lessons from running Appointment Reminder, a production Twilio application, covering testing, security, deployment, and architecture. He opens with a passionate case for Twilio as transformative technology that turns every phone into a smartphone, then pivots to the painful realities of building production telephony apps. The core of the presentation addresses a catastrophic incident where a queue failure caused 600 phone calls, 400 SMSes, and 200 emails to blast 15 people in five minutes. He advocates modeling phone calls as state machines, using staging servers, implementing strict security controls, and designing systems to fail closed rather than fail open.
key_points: Twilio's Quick Start guides teach the minimum to get phones ringing but leave developers unprepared for production realities like testing, security, and error handling
Phone calls as state machines replace brittle case statements with testable, verifiable transition tables that prevent entire categories of bugs
Telephony apps have a superset of web security issues: confidential information stops being confidential the moment you speak it into a phone
Systems should be designed to fail closed — when in doubt, don't make that phone call — because telephony errors impose real-world consequences on real people
Applications can scale orders of magnitude faster than the businesses they serve, which creates its own category of problems
money_quotes: Twilio turns every phone into a smartphone.

The end-game for Zynga's take-over-the-world vision is the human race slaved to artificial dopamine treadmills. The endgame for Twilio's vision is that every $2 handset in Africa is the moral equivalent of an iPhone.

Smartphones aren't smart because of anything on the phones themselves, they're smart because they speak HTTP and thus get always-on access to a universe of applications which are improving constantly.
This resulted in approximately 15 people receiving a total of 600 phone calls, 400 SMSes, and 200 emails, in approximately a 5 minute period of time.

Twilio testing is inherently dangerous, because non-production environments could conceivably generate actual, real-world phone calls to phone numbers which were in your database but not actually under your control.
Our brains get lulled into a false sense of security by parallel structure.
Even if your free trial is discovered by 4chan, the world should not become a darker, more evil place.
When in doubt, don't make that phone call. Design your system to fail closed.
key_insight: Production telephony applications require defensive engineering far beyond typical web apps because bugs don't just show error pages — they blast real people's phones at 3 AM.
